definition: Precision, recall, and F1 are classification metrics derived from a confusion matrix at a fixed decision threshold: precision = TP/(TP+FP) measures correctness of positive predictions; recall = TP/(TP+FN) measures coverage of actual positives; F1 is their harmonic mean 2PR/(P+R). This topic is the canonical home for these threshold-based metrics in supervised classification.
layman: After your model labels things 'positive' or 'negative,' precision asks: of everything we called positive, how many were right? Recall asks: of everything that was truly positive, how many did we catch? F1 is one fair score that punishes you if either precision or recall is bad.
content:
## Precision, recall, and F1

**Precision**, **recall**, and **F1** evaluate a **classification** model at a **single decision threshold**. They summarize how well predicted labels match ground truth for the **positive class** (or per class in multiclass settings).

This topic is the **canonical home** for **precision**, **recall**, **F1**, and the **precision–recall trade-off**. The **Confusion Matrix** topic owns the 2×2 counts **TP, FP, TN, FN**; **ROC/AUC** owns threshold-free ranking via TPR vs FPR; this topic owns **what you report once a threshold is chosen**.

### From counts to rates

Given a held-out set and a fixed rule (e.g. predict positive when score ≥ 0.5):

| Metric | Formula | Question answered |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Precision** | **TP / (TP + FP)** | Of predicted positives, what fraction are truly positive? |
| **Recall** | **TP / (TP + FN)** | Of actual positives, what fraction did we flag? |
| **F1** | **2 × (P × R) / (P + R)** | Harmonic mean balancing precision and recall |

**Recall** is also called **sensitivity**, **true positive rate (TPR)**, or **hit rate**—the y-axis of an ROC curve at one threshold. **Precision** is **positive predictive value (PPV)** at that same threshold.

### Worked binary example

Spam filter on 100 emails: **TP = 18**, **FP = 2**, **FN = 7**, **TN = 73**.

- **Precision** = 18 / (18 + 2) = **0.90** — 90% of flagged emails are spam.
- **Recall** = 18 / (18 + 7) = **0.72** — 72% of spam emails caught.
- **F1** = 2 × 0.90 × 0.72 / (0.90 + 0.72) ≈ **0.80**.

High precision, moderate recall: few false alarms, but some spam slips through.

### Precision–recall trade-off

Lowering the threshold (flag more positives) usually **raises recall** (catch more true positives) but **lowers precision** (more false positives). Raising the threshold does the opposite.

| Deployment priority | Favor | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Avoid false alarms | **Precision** | Legal doc review: wrongly flagging privileged text is costly |
| Avoid missed cases | **Recall** | Cancer screening: missing a tumor is worse than extra follow-up tests |
| Balance both | **F1** | Moderate-stakes tagging when neither error type dominates |

**ROC-AUC** compares models before picking a threshold; **precision/recall at your operating point** tells stakeholders what happens after you deploy that cutoff.

### F1 as harmonic mean

Arithmetic mean of precision and recall can look good when one metric is terrible (e.g. P=1.0, R=0.1 → arithmetic 0.55). **F1** uses the **harmonic mean**, which is dominated by the smaller value—both must be reasonable for a high F1.

Edge cases:
- If **TP = 0**, precision and recall are **0** (undefined denominators are treated as 0 in libraries).
- **Perfect** classifier: P = R = F1 = **1**.

### Multiclass extensions

For **K** classes, compute per-class precision/recall treating that class as positive and all others as negative, then aggregate:

- **Macro-F1:** mean of per-class F1 — treats rare classes equally.
- **Micro-F1:** compute global TP/FP/FN across classes, then F1 — dominated by frequent classes.
- **Weighted-F1:** macro mean weighted by class support.

Report **which averaging** you used; leaderboard numbers are not comparable otherwise.

### Relation to accuracy

**Accuracy** = (TP + TN) / total counts all correct predictions. On **imbalanced** data (e.g. 1% fraud), a model predicting all negatives can score 99% accuracy with **0 recall**. Precision, recall, and F1 focus on the **positive class** behavior that accuracy hides.

### What this topic does not cover

- **Confusion matrix layout and count definitions** → Confusion Matrix topic.
- **Threshold sweeps and ROC-AUC** → ROC/AUC topic.
- **PR-AUC** (area under precision–recall curve) → discussed alongside ROC/AUC when positives are rare; per-threshold P/R/F1 stay here.
- **Regression errors (MAE, RMSE)** → Regression vs Classification topic.
keyPoints: Precision = TP/(TP+FP); recall = TP/(TP+FN); F1 = 2PR/(P+R) at one fixed threshold.
Lowering the threshold typically raises recall and lowers precision—the operating point is a business choice.
F1 is the harmonic mean: both precision and recall must be strong for a high score.
On imbalanced data, accuracy can mislead; precision/recall expose positive-class performance.
Multiclass F1 requires stating macro, micro, or weighted averaging.
